museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Spring brings arcade holdovers, early Dragon's Lair anticipation, computer-game ambition, and a UK market increasingly defined by cassettes rather than cartridges.
museum.curatorNote: April's major moments are mostly build-up and context. Where a famous game belongs elsewhere in 1983, this file labels it as anticipation or shelf context rather than a fake April release.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: April is a waiting room full of machines.

items.title: Laserdisc arcade talk gathers force
items.body: Dragon's Lair was still ahead of its June debut, but the idea of arcade animation as an event was already forming around trade and operator attention.
items.sourceNote: Dragon's Lair debuted in June 1983; April is pre-release context.

items.title: Home computers keep absorbing game attention
items.body: As console confidence weakened, computers looked flexible: games, programming, education and magazines all in one purchase.
items.sourceNote: Crash histories describe a shift toward home computers.

items.title: Popeye tops street-location arcade charts
items.body: Nintendo's Popeye is reported at the top of Play Meter street-location charts in April, showing that character cabinets still had public pull.
items.sourceNote: Popeye references note Play Meter chart performance in April 1983.

items.title: UK micro software feels more local
items.body: The British cassette market was becoming a place where small companies and young programmers could plausibly matter.
items.sourceNote: UK micro software context ahead of Jetpac and Manic Miner.

items.title: Console discounting changes the tone of shops
items.body: In North America especially, bargain stock no longer felt like a treat. It could feel like evidence that something had gone wrong.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight April-window and anticipation objects from the archive shelf.

items.title: Popeye
items.body: A Nintendo cabinet with a clear cartoon identity and strong spring chart presence.

items.title: Mario Bros.
items.body: Reported in some North American records before its July Japanese date, making it one of the year's date-sensitive entries.
items.sourceNote: Mario Bros. release references differ by region.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Still the visual benchmark for racing cabinets.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: Character arcade design still looked energetic, not exhausted.

items.title: Dragon's Lair build-up
items.body: Not yet released, but the coming laserdisc cabinet promised something that screenshots could barely explain.

items.title: Spectrum commercial cassettes
items.body: Small British software houses kept setting the stage for the summer's landmark hits.
items.sourceNote: Specific April title date not asserted.

items.title: The Hobbit
items.body: Still a prestige adventure for UK micro owners and magazine readers.

items.title: Atari 2600 bargain games
items.body: The discount shelf became part of the story, especially in the US.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: April's objects are platforms in transition.

items.title: Arcade laserdisc hardware
items.body: Dragon's Lair would soon make a cabinet feel like a cartoon machine, even if the interaction was narrow.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: The Spectrum's low price and growing 48K audience made it fertile ground for UK game authors.

items.title: Commodore 64
items.body: Its sound and sprites made it the home computer most obviously built for games, even while UK Spectrum culture had its own identity.

items.title: Atari 2600
items.body: Still important, but increasingly framed through surplus, returns and uncertainty.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: April's paper record shows a culture between spectacle and practicality.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Arcades before the laserdisc shock
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for April 1983.
items.curatorNote: The arcade still offered enough novelty for a magazine cover, even before Dragon's Lair changed the conversation.
items.body: The arcade still offered enough novelty for a magazine cover, even before Dragon's Lair changed the conversation.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Cabinets, cassettes and machine comparisons
items.curatorNote: C&VG's mix suited a UK market where arcade admiration and home micro ownership coexisted.
items.body: C&VG's mix suited a UK market where arcade admiration and home micro ownership coexisted.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: The programmable home
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for April 1983.
items.curatorNote: The broader computing press made games part of a domestic technology story.
items.body: The broader computing press made games part of a domestic technology story.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum games from paper and tape
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for April 1983.
items.curatorNote: ZX magazines helped turn a machine into a scene.
items.body: ZX magazines helped turn a machine into a scene.

items.title: Screenshots did heavy work
items.body: For Dragon's Lair, a still image could not really explain the promise.

items.title: Listings kept games intimate
items.body: Typing a game made the machine feel less sealed and more knowable.

items.title: Retailers shaped belief
items.body: A price cut or empty shelf could change how a machine felt overnight.

items.title: Clubs filled missing knowledge
items.body: Local computer groups could turn rumours into demonstrations.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: April felt like a held breath.

items.title: Something animated was coming
items.body: Laserdisc cabinets sounded impossibly lavish.

items.title: Computers felt adaptable
items.body: If one game disappointed you, the machine could still do something else.

items.title: The UK scene felt homemade
items.body: Local adverts and cassette labels made games feel near.

items.title: The console shelf felt less innocent
items.body: A bargain was no longer automatically good news.
